sections.paragraph:
Ezr 4:8 11 Ezr 4:11, 23 Ezr 4:23, אַרְתַּחְשַׁסְתְּא Ezr 7:7, & אַרְתַּחְשֶׁשְׁתָּא 4:7 [Artaxerxes], pr.n. of several kings of Persia; in Greek written Ἀρταξέρξης, called by the Armenians արտաջէս Artashir, by the modern Persians, اردشير, اردهشير, Ardeshir, by the ancient Persians, on the inscriptions Nakshi Rustam in Niebuhr (Travels, t. ii. tab. 27), as interpreted by Silv. de Sacy, ארתחשתר Artachshetr, Artachshatra; whence, by the permutation of r and s and with the letters transposed, has sprung Artachsharta and the Hebrew Artachshast, Artachshasta.

This name is compounded of the syllable art, strong, powerful (comp. the pr. names Ἀρτοβάρης, Ἀρταβαζης, Ἀρταφέρνης), and חשתר, which in the usage of the ancient language denotes king, like the Zend. and Sansc. k‛satra, nor should we blame Herodot. (vi. 98), rendering it great warrior; for that the Persic word khshetrao, khshetria has this true and primary signification, is shewn by the Sanscr. k‛sata, one who is of the military order, a soldier. [“See Lessen, Keilschrift, p. 36.”]

Two kings of this name are mentioned in the Old Testament.-

(1) Pseudo-Smerdes, Ezr 4:7, Ezr 4:8, 23 Ezr 4:23, comp. verse 24 Ezr 4:24whom I suppose to have adopted the name of Artaxerxes, together with the regal authority.-(2) Artaxerxes Longimanus, in the seventh year of whose reign Ezra led his colony into Palestine, Ezr 7:1, Ezr 7:7, 11 Ezr 7:11, 12 Ezr 7:12, 21 Ezr 7:21 8:1 and from whose twentieth to the thirty-second, year Nehemiah governed Judæa, Neh 2:1 5:14 13:6 . See my further remarks in Thes. p. 155, 156.
